cells.title: Overview
cells.source: This notebook turns the concrete block-ordering example from `devdocs/solver-overview.md` into a runnable model. The document uses `c = d + 1` and `d = c + 1` to illustrate a cyclic component, but that pair has no finite solution. This notebook keeps the same overall teaching shape while using a convergent cyclic pair, an explicit dependency from the cyclic block back to `b`, and a lagged feedback term from `e` into `c`. That keeps the ordered blocks exactly `[a]`, `[b]`, `[c, d]`, `[e]` while also creating a more interesting period-to-period transition.

equations.name: b
equations.desc: Depends only on the solved value of a.

equations.name: c
equations.desc: First member of the simultaneous block, with current dependence on d and b plus lagged feedback from e.
equations.expression: c_weight * d + c_offset + c_from_b * b + c_from_lag_e * lag(e)

equations.name: d
equations.desc: Second member of the simultaneous block.

equations.name: e
equations.desc: Acyclic tail equation that waits for b and c.

externals.name: b_offset
externals.desc: Offset used by b = a + b_offset.

externals.name: c_weight
externals.desc: Feedback weight from d into c.

externals.name: d_weight
externals.desc: Feedback weight from c into d.

externals.name: c_from_b
externals.desc: Links the cyclic block to b so the block order matches the overview document.

externals.name: c_from_lag_e
externals.desc: Lagged feedback from e into the cyclic block to create period-to-period dynamics.

cells.title: Baseline run
cells.description: Baseline run for the block-order example using a convergent cyclic pair with lagged feedback.

cells.title: Scenario 1
cells.source: This scenario raises `c_offset` from `0.8` to `1.1` between periods 5 and 20. It shifts the cyclic block upward while leaving the acyclic equations and overall block ordering unchanged.
